name: Screenplay Beat Sheet — Story Structure, Visualized

seo.title: Screenplay Beat Sheet Board
seo.description: Blake Snyder's 15 story beats mapped as a visual board — every beat, its page number, and how the acts connect, on one shareable canvas.
seo.body: The classic 15-beat screenplay structure from Blake Snyder's Save the Cat!, laid out the way a writers' room would pin it to a corkboard: one card per beat with its target page on a 110-page script, arrows tracing the causal chain from Catalyst to Finale, and the three acts grouped so you can see the whole shape of a story at a glance. Use it as a reference while outlining, or copy the layout and fill in your own logline, beats, and scene ideas. Made with Soleil Clusters.

header.sub: Blake Snyder's 15 story beats · three acts · one canvas
Pages 1 to 25: the world before the story breaks it

cards.html:
1 · Opening Image — p. 1
A single frame that captures the hero's before state. It will be mirrored by the Final Image, so choose something that can transform.

2 · Theme Stated — p. 5
Someone tells the hero the lesson of the movie, usually in passing, usually dismissed. The rest of the script argues the point.

3 · Set-Up — pp. 1–10
Plant every character, tic, and flaw that pays off later. Show the six things that need fixing in the hero's life.

4 · Catalyst — p. 12
The telegram, the firing, the diagnosis, the knock on the door. Life as the hero knows it ends here.

5 · Debate — pp. 12–25
Can I really do this? The hero resists the call. The debate sharpens the stakes before the leap.

source.query: typewriter paper page writing close up

cards.title: FADE IN:
cards.caption: Every structure starts on page one

cards.html:
6 · Break Into Two — p. 25
The hero chooses to act and steps into the antithesis world. It must be a decision, not an accident.

7 · B Story — p. 30
The love story, the mentor, the friendship — a new relationship that carries the theme while the A story carries the plot.

8 · Fun and Games — pp. 30–55
The promise of the premise. The trailer moments. The reason the audience bought a ticket.

9 · Midpoint — p. 55
A false victory or a false defeat. Stakes are raised, the clock starts, and fun and games are over.

10 · Bad Guys Close In — pp. 55–75
External enemies regroup and internal doubts fester. The team frays. Pressure from both directions.

11 · All Is Lost — p. 75
The opposite of the Midpoint, with a whiff of death: something — a mentor, a dream, an old self — dies here.

12 · Dark Night of the Soul — pp. 75–85
The hero sits in the wreckage and finally absorbs the theme stated on page five. The lesson lands only after the loss.

source.query: person writing notebook window rain moody

cards.title: The long middle
cards.caption: Act Two is where scripts are won or abandoned

cards.html:
13 · Break Into Three — p. 85
Thanks to the B story and the theme, the hero finds the real solution — and chooses to fight with new knowledge.

14 · Finale — pp. 85–110
Five steps: gather the team, execute the plan, the high tower surprise, dig deep down, execute the new plan. The hero proves the change.

15 · Final Image — p. 110
The mirror of the Opening Image. Proof, in one frame, that transformation happened.

source.query: movie theater screen glowing dark audience

cards.title: The payoff
cards.caption: Structure the audience feels but never sees

header.sub: Beat targets on a 110-page screenplay — scale proportionally for yours

cards.html:
Where this comes from
The 15 beats are from Blake Snyder's Save the Cat! (2005), the most-used beat sheet in working screenwriting. Treat the page numbers as rhythm markers, not law — every great script bends them.
